paper_title: Transition-Aware best-of-N sampling for Longitudinal Chest X-ray Reports

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: In longitudinal clinical practice, every chest X-ray is read in the context of the patients prior exam, and much of what the radiologist communicates is the change from one visit to the next. To the best of our knowledge, we present the first training-free best-of-N sampling scheme for pre-trained chest X-ray report generators that is explicitly aware of this longitudinal prior to current transition. We call it transition-aware best-of-N sampling, each report is split into sentences and embedded into an unordered set in Rd; each (prior, current) pair is reduced to a fixed-dim directional vector via a set-to-set distance designed to encode the change between the two sets; and candidates are scored by cosine distance from their candidate transition vector to a cached bank of ground-truth training transition vectors, aggregated as min or kNN. We instantiate the framework with four directional set distances (mean-shift, novelty residual, directed-Hausdorff anchor, and cost-weighted optimal transport) and evaluate on a multi-visit AP-PA cohort, running inference under three prompts on three vision-language generators. Transition-aware best-of-N outperforms random selection across the board, with the largest relative gains on the Impression section.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: 6 2 0 2 3 2 ] . [ 1 3 9 3 8 2 . 6 0 6 2 : r Transition-Aware best-of-N sampling for Longitudinal Chest X-ray Reports H. Ibrahim Gulluk1,, Max Van Puyvelde2,3, Wim Van Criekinge3, and Olivier Gevaert2 1 Department of Electrical Engineering, Stanford University, Stanford, CA, USA gulluk@stanford.edu 2 Department of Biomedical Data Science, Stanford University School of Medicine, Stanford, CA, USA {maxvpuyv,ogevaert}@stanford.edu 3 Department of Mathematical Modelling, Statistics & Bioinformatics, Ghent University, Ghent, Belgium wim.vancriekinge@ugent.be Abstract. In longitudinal clinical practice, every chest X-ray is read in the context of the patients prior exam, and much of what the radiologist communicates is the change from one visit to the next. To the best of our knowledge, we present the first training-free best-of-N sampling scheme for pre-trained chest X-ray report generators that is explicitly aware of this longitudinal priorcurrent transition. We call it transition-aware best-of-N sampling: each report is split into sentences and embedded into an unordered set in Rd; each (prior, current) pair is reduced to fixeddim directional vector via set-to-set distance designed to encode the change between the two sets; and candidates are scored by cosine distance from their candidate transition vector to cached bank of ground-truth training transition vectors, aggregated as min or kNN. We instantiate the framework with four directional set distances (mean-shift, novelty residual, directed-Hausdorff anchor, and cost-weighted optimal transport) and evaluate on multi-visit AP/PA cohort, running inference under three prompts on three visionlanguage generators. Transition-aware bestof-N outperforms random selection across the board, with the largest relative gains on the Impression section. Keywords: Chest X-ray Report Generation Longitudinal Imaging Best-of-N Sampling Set-to-Set Distance Transition Modelling.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Chest radiography is the most frequently performed imaging modality in medicine, and automating chest X-ray report generation can substantially ease radiologist workload while improving reporting consistency. Modern visionlanguage models (VLMs) can already draft credible reports [13,10,7,2,4], and simple training-free Equal contribution. 2 H. I. Gulluk et al. way to extract further quality from such generators is best-of-N sampling: drawing candidate reports per image and selecting the one that is the best in terms if the preferences or the performance. The vast majority of recent chest X-ray report generators and the corresponding best-of-N pipelines built on top of them treat each test image in isolation, scoring candidates against distribution of single-image reference reports [8,2,4]. In real clinical practice, however, chest X-rays are rarely read in isolation: most exams are acquired as follow-ups, and the radiologists report is largely organised around how the patient has changed since the prior study (stable interval, new opacity, improved aeration). Modelling this longitudinal context has been shown to improve generation quality when fed to the decoder [3,14], but to our knowledge the same idea has not yet been exploited at the selection stage of best-of-N pipeline. We argue that for longitudinal image the appropriate notion of best response should be selected based on the transitions from the prior visit to the current visit. As some transitions might not be clinically valid although those can be generated by language model. We turn this intuition into concrete pipeline. Each report is split into sentences and embedded into an unordered set in Rd by frozen sentence transformer (Sec. 3.1, Fig. 1). directional set-to-set distance then reduces each (prior, current) pair to single fixed-dim vector (Sec. 3.2, Fig. 2 (a)). bank of such ground-truth training transition vectors is cached once. At test time, each candidate report becomes its own transition vector and is cosine-scored against the bank (Fig. 2 (b)). Crucially, an individually plausible-looking candidate report can nonetheless describe clinically implausible change from the patients prior study e.g. silently dropping chronic finding or hallucinating an acute worsening that the image does not support. Scoring in transition space penalises exactly these candidates, because their displacement from the prior departs from the displacements observed in training; report-space scoring, by contrast, only checks that the candidate looks like some real report. The transition bank therefore provides prior on how reports change between visits, not just on what individual reports look like. We instantiate this idea with small family of directional set distances, and benchmark it on multi-visit cohort drawn from publicly available chest X-ray dataset across several visionlanguage generators and prompting regimes; transition-aware best-of-N outperforms random selection on wide range of text-overlap and clinical-content metrics.

sections.title: 2 Related work
sections.content: Early systems combined CNN encoder with an LSTM/Transformer decoder [13,10] or retrieved canned sentences from memory of training reports [7]. More recent work pairs domain-specific visual encoders with general-purpose LLMs: R2GenGPT [21] aligns Swin-Transformer visual feature stream to frozen LLaMA-2 via learned projection; CheXagent [4] instruction-tunes clinical LLM jointly with CXR vision encoder on curated mixture of 28 CXR Transition-Aware best-of-N sampling for Longitudinal Chest X-ray Reports 3 datasets; MAIRA-2 [2] couples the RAD-DINO image encoder with Vicuna-7B and per-finding grounding head to produce spatially grounded reports. Several recent works exploit the patients prior study at generation time. BioViL-T [3] self-supervises CNN+ViT encoder that explicitly fuses current and prior CXR pairs and is used for temporal classification, phrase grounding and longitudinal report generation. The authors [14] condition the report decoder on the prior study (handling its absence with learned placeholder) and add CXR-BERT semantic-similarity reward during fine-tuning. Both target the decoder; in contrast we operate downstream at the best-of-N selection stage and require no changes to the generator. Representing report as an unordered set of sentence embeddings is natural fit for chest X-ray reading: standard set-to-set distances Chamfer, Hausdorff, optimal transport [20], Hungarian matching [9] yield continuous, permutationinvariant scores between two reports. The closest existing recipe to ours is SDR [8], which uses such sentence-set distances both as GRPO reward and as the scorer for single-image best-of-N selection; it remains image-independent and does not encode change between visits. Best-of-N sampling with learned verifier is standard test-time scaling tool in the LLM literature [5,11]: candidates are drawn from stochastic generator and the highest-scoring one is kept. Our pipeline has the same shape but the scorer operates in transition space rather than at the candidate level.

sections.title: 4 Experimental setup
sections.content: 4.1 Multi-visit cohort We derive multi-visit AP/PA cohort from ReXGradient-160K [23], keeping patients with at least two qualifying exams, sampling single view (AP or PA) per patient, and forming one transition per consecutive visit pair. The 22,745 training transitions populate the bank BS ; the 1,727 test transitions are the evaluation set. Per-split counts are in Appendix C. Transition-Aware best-of-N sampling for Longitudinal Chest X-ray Reports 7 Table 1: Impressions headline metrics averaged across the runs in which every listed method has data. Subscripts give the relative change vs. the random baseline row at the bottom (italicised). The best transition row per column is in bold. The very last row reports the per-metric mean across all transition methods. Full prompt texts are in Appendix A. For each (model, prompt) configuration and each test transition we sample = 5 candidate reports with distinct seeds. 4.3 Evaluation metrics Selected responses are scored against the current-visit ground-truth report with the standard combination of NLP overlap metrics and clinical-content metrics, separately for Findings and Impressions; the full list of metrics and references is given in Appendix D. The random baseline is uniform pick over the five candidates, averaged over five seeds.

sections.title: 5 Results
sections.content: 5.1 Headline metrics averaged across runs Tables 1 and 2 report the seven headline metrics on Impressions and Findings, averaged across all 9 (model, prompt) configurations. The best transition row per column is in bold; subscripts give the relative change versus random. Impressions. Transition-aware best-of-N delivers substantial relative gains on Impressions: the strongest configurations give +8.5% ROUGE-L (Novelty/kNN), +13.6% RadGraph averaged-F1 (Mean-Shift/min), and +4.4% METEOR (DirHausdorff/kNN). Impressions are short, change-oriented summary statements e.g. stable since prior, new opacity in the right lower lobe and match the regime in which transition signal should carry the most ranking information. 8 H. Every min and kNN aggregation of every transition vector beats random on every headline metric. The strongest result on BERTScore-F1 (+3.1%) and RadGraph averaged-F1 (+5.1%) is from Cost-OT/min; the best CheXbert F-14 is from Mean-Shift/kNN (+2.2%). 5.2 Per-model and per-prompt breakdowns Appendices and break the averaged numbers down by (model, prompt). Transition-aware best-of-N beats random on nearly every Impressions configuration and on the majority of Findings ones, with the largest gains on the weaker prompts and consistent trend across both Gemini and Mistral.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: We introduced transition-aware best-of-N sampling for chest X-ray report generation. Each (prior, current) report pair is reduced to single fixed-dim vector by directional set-to-set distance, and candidates are scored at test time against cached bank of ground-truth training transition vectors. To our knowledge this is the first training-free best-of-N scheme for pre-trained chest X-ray report generators that explicitly conditions the scorer on the patients prior exam. Across nine (model, prompt) configurations on multi-visit AP/PA cohort the pipeline consistently beats random selection on both report sections, with the largest relative gains on the change-oriented Impressions and stable trend across both Gemini and Mistral generators. Several extensions are worth pursuing: learnt directional encoders trained to maximise alignment with target clinical metric, larger candidate pools , longer histories beyond single prior visit, and combining prior-conditioned generation with prior-conditioned selection as two complementary ways of injecting longitudinal context. Transition-Aware best-of-N sampling for Longitudinal Chest X-ray Reports

sections.title: A Prompts
sections.content: We reproduce verbatim the three prompts used in the experiments. The few-shot examples in P2 are sampled at random from the training corpus for every test query; the placeholders {prior_findings} and {prior_impression} in P3 are filled at query time with the patients prior ground-truth Findings and Impression respectively. P1 Zero-shot, image-only prompt1.txt You are radiology report generation model specialized in chest X-rays. Generate concise clinical report for the given image. Strict requirements: - Output ONLY in this exact format: Findings: <text> Impression: <text> - Do NOT include explanations, disclaimers, or any extra text. - Do NOT include phrases such as consult doctor or this is not medical advice. - Use professional radiology language. - Keep it concise and structured. Output: P2 Few-shot with random training examples prompt2.txt You are radiology report generation model. Given chest X-ray image, generate concise radiology report. Follow these strict rules: - Output ONLY in this format: Findings: <text> Impression: <text> - Do NOT include any explanations, disclaimers, or additional commentary. - Do NOT say things like consult doctor or this is not medical advice. - Match the writing style, tone, and structure of the examples below. - Be concise and clinically accurate. Here are example reports: Example 0: Findings: Mild cardiomegaly. No edema. No consolidation or effusion. No pneumothorax. H. I. Gulluk et al. Impression: Mild cardiomegaly Example 1: Findings: No pneumonia is seen. Minimal peribronchial thickening is noted. The heart is within normal limits in size. No bony abnormality is seen. Impression: No pneumonia. Mild peribronchial thickening. Example 2: Findings: The heart size and mediastinal contours are within normal limits. Both lungs are clear. The visualized skeletal structures are unremarkable. Impression: No active cardiopulmonary disease. Example 3: Findings: The lungs are well-expanded. The interstitial markings are increased bilaterally. Patchy areas of confluence are noted in the mid to lower left lung and at the right lung base. The heart and pulmonary vascularity are normal. The mediastinum is normal in width. There is multilevel degenerative disc disease of the thoracic spine. Impression: Bilateral interstitial pneumonia with patchy areas of alveolar infiltrate. No pulmonary edema. No pleural effusion. Followup PA and lateral chest X-ray is recommended in 3-4 weeks following trial of antibiotic therapy to ensure resolution and exclude underlying malignancy. Example 4: Findings: The heart size and mediastinal contours are within normal limits. Both lungs are clear. The visualized skeletal structures are unremarkable. Impression: No active disease. Now generate the report for the given chest X-ray image. Output: P3 Zero-shot with prior report as context prompt3.txt You are radiology report generation model specialized in chest X-rays. You are given chest X-ray image of patient who has at least one prior chest X-ray exam. Use the patients prior report below as clinical context, and then generate the report for the new image describing what is present now, including any changes (new findings, worsening, improvement, resolution) relative to the prior. Patients prior chest X-ray report: Findings: {prior_findings} Impression: {prior_impression} Transition-Aware best-of-N sampling for Longitudinal Chest X-ray Reports 13 Strict requirements: - Output ONLY in this exact format: Findings: <text> Impression: <text> - Do NOT include explanations, disclaimers, or any extra text. - Do NOT include phrases such as consult doctor or this is not medical advice. - Do NOT copy the prior report verbatim describe the new image, noting changes. - Use professional radiology language. - Keep it concise and structured. Output: 14 H. I. Gulluk et al. Table 3: Directional set distances used in this work. Each maps two sentence-embedding sets A, Rd to single fixed-dim vector that can be cosine-compared across transitions. µ() denotes the mean of the set; NNA(b) the cosine-nearest neighbour of in A; and γ the optimal-transport plan between the uniform measures on and with cost matrix Mij = d(ai, bj). Output dim is for all four. Name Vector Mean-Shift Novelty bB Dir-Hausdorff Tdh(A, B) = NNA(b), b= arg maxbB minaA d(b, a) Cost-OT Tms(A, B) = µ(B) µ(A) Tnov(A, B) = 1 (cid:0)b NNA(b)(cid:1) Tcot(A, B) = ij Mij (bj ai) i,j γ

sections.title: B Directional set distances
sections.content: Table 3 compactly summarises the four directional set-to-set distances defined in Sec. 3.2 (Mean-Shift, Novelty, Dir-Hausdorff, Cost-OT). Each maps pair of sentence-embedding sets to single fixed-dim vector that can be cosinecompared across transitions. Transition-Aware best-of-N sampling for Longitudinal Chest X-ray Reports 15 Table 4: Multi-visit AP/PA chest X-ray cohort. Only patients with 2 qualifying exams are kept; one view (AP or PA) is chosen per patient. Transitions are the (k1, k) visit pairs that drive the transition bank and the test queries. Split Patients Visits Transitions train 19,492 42,237 1,411 3,090 valid 1,459 3,186 test 22,745 1,679 1,727 Multi-visit cohort statistics Table 4 reports per-split patient, visit and transition counts for the multi-visit AP/PA cohort used throughout Sec. 4.1. 16 H. I. Gulluk et al.

sections.title: D Evaluation metrics in detail
sections.content: Selected responses are scored against the current-visit ground-truth report with the following suite, separately for Findings and Impressions: Sentenceand corpus-BLEU at = 1, . . . ,4 [15]. ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2 and ROUGE-L, each as precision, recall and F-measure [12]. METEOR [1]. BERTScore-F1 with roberta-large, rescaled with baseline [22]. COMET (wmt22-comet-da) [17]. chrF++ [16]. RadGraph entity, entity-relation and averaged F1 [6]. CheXbert F1 at 14 classes and 5 classes [19]. The seven scores used in the headline tables (BLEU-1, ROUGE-1 F-measure, ROUGE-L F-measure, METEOR, BERTScore-F1, RadGraph averaged-F1, CheXbert F-14) are representative subset spanning shallow text-overlap (BLEU1, ROUGE), soft lexical/semantic similarity (METEOR, BERTScore-F1), and clinical-content correctness (RadGraph, CheXbert). Per-(model, prompt) absolute values Tables 5 and 6 report the absolute headline values of the strongest single transition method (Novelty/min) against the random baseline, one row per (model, prompt) configuration. Three patterns hold across the nine runs: Transition-aware best-of-N beats the matched random baseline on every (model, prompt) configuration for ROUGE-L and RadGraph averaged-F1 on Impressions, and on the majority of configurations on Findings. The largest absolute gains appear on the weaker prompts P1 and P2; under P3 (prior already in the prompt) the random baseline starts from substantially higher absolute value and the method gain compresses but does not invert. The gain pattern is consistent across model families: both Gemini and Mistral see positive deltas, indicating that the transition signal is not tied to single generators failure modes. 18 H. I. Gulluk et al. Table 6: Per-(model, prompt) absolute values on Findings. Strongest transition method (Novelty / min) vs. the random baseline; subscript is % change vs. random.
